flashcards.front: What is Demand Paging in Virtual Memory?
flashcards.back: Demand paging is a virtual memory management technique where pages are loaded into memory only when requested by a CPU reference (page fault). This approach defers page loading until actual need, reducing I/O operations and memory usage.

flashcards.front: List the four major page replacement algorithms.
flashcards.back:
1. FIFO (First In First Out) - Replaces oldest page in memory
2. Optimal (OPT) - Replaces page that won't be used longest
3. LRU (Least Recently Used) - Replaces least recently accessed page
4. LFU (Least Frequently Used) - Replaces least frequently accessed page

flashcards.front: Define Effective Access Time (EAT) in demand paging with formula.
flashcards.back: EAT is the average time to access memory considering page fault probability. Formula: EAT = (1 - p) × ma + p × page fault time, where p = page fault rate, ma = memory access time. If page fault service time = m, then EAT ≈ (1-p)×ma + p×m.

flashcards.front: What is Thrashing? What causes it?
flashcards.back: Thrashing occurs when a system spends more time paging (swapping pages in/out) than executing processes, due to insufficient allocated frames. High page fault rate → more paging I/O → CPU idle → OS thinks more processes needed → more memory demand →恶性循环.

Belady's Anomaly is the counterintuitive phenomenon where increasing the number of page frames can actually increase the number of page faults in FIFO replacement algorithm. This occurs because FIFO doesn't consider actual page usage patterns.

flashcards.front: Calculate EAT: Memory access time = 100ns, page fault service time = 10ms (10,000,000ns), page fault rate = 0.05.
flashcards.back:
EAT = (1-0.05)×100ns + 0.05×10,000,000ns
EAT = 0.95×100 + 0.05×10,000,000
EAT = 95 + 500,000
EAT = 500,095ns ≈ 500μs
Note: Page fault service time dominates; even small p drastically increases EAT.

flashcards.front: Why is the Optimal (OPT) page replacement algorithm not implementable in practice?
flashcards.back: OPT requires future knowledge of which pages will be referenced, which is impossible in real systems. It serves as a theoretical benchmark to evaluate other algorithms. Real implementations use approximations like LRU or working-set models.

flashcards.front: Difference between Pure Demand Paging and Prepaging.
flashcards.back: Pure Demand Paging: Loads pages ONLY when page fault occurs (high initial page faults). Prepaging: Loads more pages than requested in advance, anticipating future references. Prepaging reduces page faults but may waste memory if predicted pages aren't used.
